data.title: Booking Holdings: A Chance To Buy The Dip On Bookings Strength (Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the past few weeks, the main headlines that have moved stocks without fail are updates on the war. Soaring gas prices are carrying the risk of macroeconomic disruption on top of an already shaky domestic macro picture.

So perhaps it's needless to say that travel stocks have also been deeply impacted. Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG), the largest OTA (online travel agency) platform, has seen its share price slide nearly ~20% since the start of the year. The question for investors now is, is it an appropriate time to de-risk our portfolios given the possibility for disruption here, or is this a good opportunity to add Booking on the dip?

I last wrote a “Sell” article on Booking in November, when the stock was trading in the low $5,000s. With strong bookings since then and a reduced valuation, I think investors should look past the near-term disruptions and focus on Booking's long-term appeal. That's especially true as the company's bookings growth trends have begun to strengthen relative to peers. As a result, I'm upgrading Booking to a “Buy” rating here.

In my view, there are many reasons to “Buy” Booking, especially at newly discounted prices. These are the top drivers of the bull case for the company:

  • The travel industry has a history of catching up after a period of disruption. There is no doubt that the combination of the Iran war, which is cutting off many critical routes between Europe and Asia, plus spiking oil prices that have led to higher airfares, will disrupt travel in the near term. However, the pattern from COVID-19 suggests that travelers still build up a “backlog” and continue to take their trips after conditions return to normal (which was termed “revenge travel” in the post-COVID era).
  • Strongest bookings among peers. Booking Holdings and its group of sites (which includes Booking.com, Priceline, Agoda, and others) is the largest OTA and is also the fastest-growing. Despite being ~50% larger than Expedia in scale, gross bookings growth rates in the most recent quarter outperformed Expedia by 5 points.
  • Diversifying supply. The company also continues to add to its inventory beyond hotel rooms, such as vacation rental homes, to compete against Airbnb. The company now has 8.6 million listings of “alternative accommodations,” up 8% y/y.
  • Focus on cost controls. Booking has flattened its headcount growth, which is enabling meaningful adjusted EBITDA and EPS expansion.

From a valuation perspective, while it's true that Booking remains the premium stock in the OTA space, we find its valuation multiples quite reasonable. Today, its 12x forward adjusted EBITDA ratio and its 16x forward P/E multiples still sit below Airbnb (which has a similar high-teens gross bookings growth pace).

Moreover, Booking now trades at a discount to the broader S&P 500. Again, while we cannot completely ignore the risk of travel disruption from a protracted war, we do think that once the conflict resolves, Booking will benefit from catch-up demand.

Go long here: In my view, this is a great buying opportunity in a perennially expensive and fundamentally strong business.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Booking's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Booking's revenue grew 16% y/y to $6.35 billion, substantially beating Wall Street's expectations of $6.13 billion (+12% y/y) by a meaningful four-point margin. Furthermore, revenue growth accelerated three points relative to 13% y/y growth in Q3.

Revenue growth, in turn, was also fueled by recent strength in gross bookings. As a reminder, OTA companies recognize gross bookings when customers book their trips, while revenue is recognized later when trips are actually taken (which may cause delayed revenue recognition for trips booked further out in advance). In Q4, Booking's gross bookings grew at 16% y/y (with five points of upside coming from favorable FX rates, namely the weaker U.S. dollar) and accelerated two points versus Q3.

Again, one of the more important points here to note is how Booking is trending versus peers. The travel industry overall had held up well in 2025 despite the shakier macro, and Booking most of all: its bookings growth rate outpaced its main rival Expedia by five points in Q4, with Expedia's bookings growth rate actually slowing sequentially in the quarter.

Speaking to in-quarter demand trends from customers on the Q4 earnings call, CFO Ewout Steenbergen noted as follows:

Our room nights in the fourth quarter grew 9%, exceeding the high end of our guidance range by 3 percentage points as we saw benefits from our continued investments into Asia and the U.S., and the booking window was more expanded than expected. On a regional basis, Asia and the U.S. each delivered low double-digit growth, and Europe and the Rest of World were up high single digits. We're pleased to see an acceleration in U.S. room night growth over a strong prior year comparison.

Growth in the U.S. was helped by continued targeted investments, including in brands and our traditional performance marketing channels, and by continued momentum in our B2B business. We're also encouraged by the continued growth in our direct channel in the U.S. The booking window in the U.S. remained steady in the fourth quarter, though we continue to see slightly lower ADRs and a slightly shorter length of stay versus the prior year, which may indicate that some consumer segments are continuing to be thoughtful on their discretionary spending.

Perhaps even more heartening is the fact that Booking is expected to continue top-line strength and healthy travel demand in FY26. Its guidance for FY26 calls for low double-digit growth in both revenue and gross bookings (despite a tough comp versus very solid performance in FY25). The company is also implying adjusted EBITDA margin expansion and healthy mid-teens earnings growth. Of course, the major question here is how the current Iran conflict will play into these expectations (we do expect some near-term revisions next quarter), but I also think the impacts may be short-lived.

Another impressive item is that, despite Booking's top-line strength, the company has also been laser-focused on moderating expenses, too. As of the end of Q4, the company actually shed 500 net employees, ending the year with 24.3k employees: flat y/y.

It appears that Booking is relying on natural attrition to bring headcount levels down and leaning on automation and technology to make workstreams more efficient. Q4 adjusted EBITDA grew 19% y/y to $2.20 billion, hitting a 34.6% margin that improved 80 bps y/y, while pro forma EPS of $48.80 grew 17% y/y and beat Street expectations of $48.67.

Key takeaways

It's understandable that investors have sold off Booking Holdings on intensifying war conditions, but I also think the selloff creates an opportunity to "Buy" into a very attractive growth stock at just ~16x P/E. Use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: CHAT: Pure-Play Exposure To GenAI Revolution
data.description:
The Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT) offers a concentrated portfolio of firms positioned at the cutting edge of the generative AI revolution, spanning the full stack from foundational model developers and cloud hyperscalers to the semiconductor infrastructure that powers them. The fund's portfolio blends high-growth U.S. technology giants, most prominently Alphabet (GOOG), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Microsoft (MSFT), with emerging international players like China's MiniMax Group, giving it a global dimension that few thematic AI peers can match. Unlike broader technology ETFs, CHAT makes no attempt to hedge its thematic conviction: it is a pure-play bet on the commercialization of generative AI.

This article will assess the ETF’s short-term and long-term drivers, risks, valuation, and investment case.

The Fund

CHAT is Roundhill’s actively managed, thematic generative-AI ETF, which was launched on May 18, 2023. It aims to achieve long-term capital appreciation through investments in companies participating in the generative AI revolution, including platforms engaged in the development and commercialization of large language models and infrastructure companies providing hardware and semiconductor solutions designed and optimized for the use case of AI. Unlike the broader technology indices, CHAT has a high-conviction portfolio consisting of 40 to 50 names. Its active management philosophy has enabled it to shift into high-performance segments like High Bandwidth Memory and Edge Computing.

The weighting or exposure score, as Roundhill addresses, is somewhat unique and based on the expected financial performance emerging from generative AI for the respective company. The more the company is expected to derive its profits or revenue or R&D from generative AI, the higher score it gets. The higher the score, the higher the weight. Minimum requirements are at least $250 million in market capitalization and at least $500k in average daily trading volume, which means that a company from any capitalization qualification can be an eligible constituent, although the current average market cap of a CHAT constituent is around $1.3 trillion. You can read more about the investment strategy as well as portfolio construction in the fund prospectus here.

We’ll continue with Seeking Alpha ratings, giving an overview of some fund characteristics. The fund has a Seeking Alpha D+ Expense Grade, with an expense ratio of 75 basis points higher than the 0.50% of the median ETF. Actively managed funds typically command higher expense ratios, and I would not say this is abnormal for the fund to have an expense ratio higher than the median. Regarding liquidity, the fund has a high Seeking Alpha B+ Grade, with high daily share and dollar volume of 326 thousand shares and around $20.3 million traded daily. Assets under management are also relatively high, at around $1 billion.

The sector allocation is heavily concentrated in the Technology and Communication sectors, with 74% and 17%, respectively. The third and fourth sectors in the fund structure are Consumer Cyclical with 6.6% and Industrials with the leftover 1%.

The fund has diversified international exposure, while the bulk of the fund is represented by the United States, with around 66% of the fund’s assets, followed by China with 9%, South Korea with around 7%, and Taiwan with 5%. The rest of the fund’s assets are invested in companies domiciled in the Netherlands, Hong Kong, Japan, Britain, and Canada.

Compared to peers in the industry, CHAT is generally comparable to other peer funds and stands out in the Momentum category. Dividends and Expenses are categories in which CHAT is lagging behind peers such as Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AIQ), ROBO Global Artificial Intelligence ETF (THNQ), and WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence and Innov Fund ETF (WTAI), while Liquidity and Risk grades are similar to peers.

Regarding performance, CHAT has had the best performance on a 3-year time series. I picked this time frame as it has corresponded with CHAT’s inception date.

CapEx Spending, Increasing GenAI Adoption, and Perspectives of Agentic AI

The fund's growth narrative is underpinned by a solid foundation of short-term drivers and structural long-term trends. In the short term, the fund benefits from the "GTC 2026 Effect" in which NVIDIA announced the Vera CPU and Vera Rubin inference systems, which have forecasted chip orders to reach $1 trillion by 2027. This infrastructure supercycle also benefits from hyperscalers' capital expenditures, which are a significant driver for a fund like CHAT given the number of constituents in the AI supply chain. In fact, Alphabet reported capex in 2026 could potentially double as the company looks to alleviate compute constraints. Moreover, Meta (META) increased capex for 2026 by 73% to $115-$135 billion as the company looks to pursue its "superintelligence" initiative. In fact, global AI spend for the "Big Tech" group is expected to be $700 billion in 2026, up substantially from 2025. Morgan Stanley estimates a $2.9 trillion global investment cycle in data centers for the period between 2025 and 2028, a staggering figure if it materializes, lifting the entire sector.

Investors are rewarding those companies that are making their AI investments pay off and are punishing those that are not. This provides a clear near-term “barometer” for CHAT constituents such as Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Nvidia, Broadcom (AVGO), Arista (ANET), SK hynix (HXSC.F), Oracle (ORCL), and the newer infrastructure names in the portfolio. This setup provides a tailwind for incremental upside in case enterprise spending continues to be robust, cloud spending accelerates again, and AI infrastructure remains capacity constrained. On the demand side, the driver is now no longer “AI excitement” but actual usage. In fact, GenAI adoption rates have almost doubled to 40% of professionals from 22% last year. More than 80% of existing users are utilizing it at least once a week, and 90%+ expect it to be central to their workflow in five years or less.

Long term, CHAT sits at the crossroads of several structural tailwinds. Perhaps the most important of these is the development of agentic AI infrastructure. In this space, there are forecasts that 40% of enterprise applications will be integrated with task-specific AI agents by the end of 2026, up from fewer than 5% in 2025. Furthermore, developer adoption of agentic frameworks grew by a staggering 920% year-over-year. This explosive growth in infrastructure demand maintains the capex supercycle for NVIDIA and the hyperscalers in the fund. From an economic perspective, there are forecasts that AI agents could drive $2.6 to $4.4 trillion in annual value across business use cases.

Also relevant here is the global scope; the meaningful investment by CHAT in non-U.S. equities, such as the Chinese AI companies like MiniMax Group, allows it to benefit from the investment returns generated by the competitive and increasingly complex global AI supply chain.

Valuation

Fund valuation seemingly is not as expensive as I would have expected, given the high-growth expectation of constituents. The fund is trading at a P/E of 20.51x, lower than both the S&P 500 and NASDAQ multiples of 22.41x and 25.88x.

Other multiples such as P/B and P/S are also lower than the peer group, as per Morningstar, while long-term earnings are double-digit. Cash flow growth as well as historical earnings growth is far superior to the peer group.

Risks

The key risks are concentration, valuation, especially one concerning a potential AI bubble, and policy/region-related risks. It is a concentrated fund in technology and is an explicitly non-diversified fund, so if there is a selloff in semis, cloud software, or mega-cap platforms, CHAT will be more impacted than a broader ETF, as its top 10 holdings are around 45% of assets, and large-cap equities dominate this fund. It also has significant non-U.S. exposure in names from Korea, Taiwan, China, the Netherlands, and others, so there is currency, geopolitical, and regulatory risk.

The AI bubble discussion remains a relevant and serious issue, as a study released in February 2026 by the National Bureau of Economic Research found that 90% of firm CEOs surveyed indicated that AI did not have a significant impact on workplace productivity, but executives still expect significant AI-driven productivity gains, potentially implying that valuations are rising well ahead of actual realized economic benefits.

Some industry observers increasingly point out that there are several hyperscalers building similar models, developing similar infrastructure, and competing for the same constrained resources, which could potentially result in poor returns on capital. Regulatory risks are also rising, as it was announced that as of August 2, 2026, companies must comply with specific transparency requirements and rules for high-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act, and U.S. state-level legislation continues to expand.

Conclusion

CHAT, in my opinion, presents a well-constructed, actively managed fund for a concentrated, high-conviction exposure to the Gen AI trend. The companies in the portfolio represent a play on the platform layer of AI as well as the infrastructure layer. Moreover, the international scope of the portfolio, including Chinese AI names, gives this fund a dimension not available in a purely U.S.-focused investment. The investment thesis has long-term potential: agentic AI deployment is accelerating, AI usage is increasing in virtually every industry, and the economic opportunity size is measured in trillions of dollars.

That said, the fund is highly volatile and not for the faint of heart. Fund structure is highly concentrated thematically, making it extremely sensitive to any change in investor sentiment on AI, regulatory hurdles, or a slowdown in the monetization. Therefore, I would rate the fund a BUY from a long-term perspective, it being a selective bet on the GenAI process, while expecting volatility arising from the covered risks in the short to medium term.

data.title: Invesco Municipal Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Fund performance

Invesco Municipal Income Fund Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) underperformed its style-specific index, the S&P Municipal Bond 5+ Year Investment Grade Index.

2 Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco (IVZ) Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

3 Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In the fourth quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 1.42%, 1.11% and 1.05%, with annual returns of 4.25%, 2.46% and 7.89%, respectively. 1 Despite a lengthy federal government shutdown, long-duration municipal bonds performed well during the quarter, bolstering market strength. 1
  • New municipal issuance reached $143 billion for the quarter and a record $584 billion for the year, surpassing last year's record $509 billion. Shifting interest rate policies, higher costs and political uncertainty likely encouraged more issuers to come to market. 1
  • Net flows for municipal mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) were strong, totaling approximately $17.5 billion for the quarter and $52.4 billion for the year. 2
  • The US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the federal funds rate twice, by 0.25% in October and 0.25% in December. The Fed reiterated its commitment to balancing maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. 3
  • We believe state and local municipal budgets remain healthy. While credit rating upgrades have moderated, upgrades outpaced downgrades in 2025, demonstrating to us strong fundamentals. 4
  • Our experienced credit research team continues to seek out market dislocations, uncovering opportunities to add value for shareholders.
  • Looking ahead, we see attractive opportunities in municipals. With prospects for more Fed interest rate cuts, steady issuance and ongoing demand for tax-exempt income, we believe high absolute yields and solid fundamentals make municipals a compelling choice.

Portfolio positioning

Yield Curve and Duration Positioning: Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook on duration and see attractive opportunities to increase duration following the steepening of the municipal yield curve in 2025. We plan to selectively add longer duration securities to the fund when appropriate, having found particular value in the long end of the yield curve, specifically maturities of 15 to 20 years.

Sector Allocations: We maintain an overweight position in revenue bonds relative to state and local general obligation bonds, though we note that pension risks historically associated with general obligation bonds have generally been mitigated over the past several years. Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the airport, hospital and industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR) sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market, as credit rating upgrades have continued to outpace downgrades. While various sectors have come under pressure due to reduced federal support, inflation-driven cost pressures and negative political headlines, we continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to identify strong credits with attractive yields.

Performance highlights

The fourth quarter began on a strong note despite record levels of new issuance and the longest federal government shutdown in history. Economic signals were mixed, with solid Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth alongside persistent inflation and a weakening employment outlook. The Fed cut the federal funds target rate by 0.25% in both October and December, for a total of three rate cuts in 2025. Municipals rallied in October and delivered steady, positive returns through the end of the quarter. Yields on the longer end of the municipal yield curve declined, with 10- and 30-year bond yields falling by 0.19% and 0.11%, respectively. 5 Overall, higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed during the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

The fund's underweight in and security selection among bonds with durations between three and seven years contributed to the fund's relative performance. An overweight allocation and security selection in lifecare bonds also boosted relative return during the quarter.

Detractors from performance

Detractors from relative return this quarter included the following:

Security selection in non-rated IDR/PCR and transportation bonds detracted from relative performance during the quarter. Additionally, security selection among zero-coupon tobacco bonds detracted from relative return.

Settlement payments are based on factors, including, but not limited to, annual domestic cigarette shipments, cigarette consumption, inflation and the financial capability of participating tobacco companies. data.title: Middle East Conflict: Central Bank Forecast Changes
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Geopolitical backdrop

Tensions between the U.S. and Iran have escalated sharply, marked by military exchanges and increasingly confrontational rhetoric. Attacks on key Middle Eastern energy infrastructure have heightened the risk of material supply disruptions, amplifying uncertainty across global markets. While there are tentative signs of de-escalation—including reports that President Trump has ordered a five-day pause on planned strikes against Iranian power and energy facilities—the situation remains highly fluid, with risks of retaliation and broader supply chain disruption still elevated.

Market reaction

The escalation has triggered a sell-off in global equities, most acute in Asia, where economies are highly exposed to Middle Eastern energy supply. European equities have also fallen meaningfully as investors reassess growth and inflation risks. U.S. equities, while weaker, have been comparatively more resilient.

The most pronounced reaction has been in bond markets. Following recent central bank meetings, markets have moved decisively to price a more hawkish global policy response, with yields rising sharply as investors reassess the risk that energy-driven inflation proves persistent enough to warrant tighter policy.

If the shock endures, central banks face increasingly difficult trade-offs. Prolonged energy price pressures sharpen the inflation–growth dilemma, as restoring inflation to target would require a more restrictive stance at a higher cost to growth and employment. In the U.S., higher energy investment may partially offset the drag, but near-term pressure on real incomes is likely to dominate.

Central bank outlook – critical for markets

History suggests U.S. equities tend to struggle most during geopolitical crises when the Federal Reserve tightens policy despite deteriorating financial conditions. In such episodes, policy tightening has typically weighed more heavily on returns than the geopolitical shock itself.

Since the oil shocks of the 1970s and 1980s, the Fed has generally avoided tightening in response to supply-driven inflation, aided by inflation targeting and improved central bank credibility. The key exception was 2022, when central banks tightened aggressively following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, though inflation pressures were already elevated due to post-pandemic supply constraints and expansive fiscal policy.

That episode matters. Central banks were widely criticized for responding too late, and with inflation remaining above target for several years in many economies, the threshold for responding to a sustained energy price shock has likely been lowered.

Scenarios: from baseline to adverse

At the onset of the conflict, we outlined three scenarios for higher energy prices:

Baseline: A brief oil spike with negligible growth effects and a temporary ~0.2ppt lift to headline inflation, with little policy impact.

Medium adverse: Oil stabilizes around $90/bbl for several months, modestly slowing growth and pushing core inflation toward 3%—constraining rate cuts.

Severe adverse (tail risk): A multi-quarter disruption, potentially including a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, drives Brent oil above $125/bbl, materially weakens growth, lifts core inflation above 3.5%—raising the odds of policy tightening.

Recent developments move us out of the baseline and toward a point between the medium and severe adverse scenarios. Brent oil prices are now likely to stabilize in a $90–$115/bbl range for several months, with widening supply chain risks as damage to Gulf energy infrastructure accumulates. As a result, we’ve made revisions to our Fed, ECB, and BoE policy projections.

At the start of the year, markets expected rate cuts from the Fed, ECB, and BoE. In recent weeks, pricing has swung sharply toward hikes across all three—a dramatic repricing. Our own starting point for 2026 had been two Fed cuts in H2, no ECB cuts, and two BoE cuts.

Last week, major central banks held rates steady but emphasised upside inflation risks alongside concerns about demand destruction. While the tone was broadly hawkish, urgency differed: the Fed was least hawkish, the Bank of England most.

Federal Reserve

Original forecast: Two cuts (June and September)

Revised view: Cuts delayed

The Fed’s dual mandate implies sensitivity to both inflation risks and demand destruction. We expect the FOMC to largely look through the rise in headline inflation and, unlike markets, we do not anticipate rate hikes this year. Labor market softening and affordability pressures argue for an eventual return toward neutral.

However, with inflation having remained above target for five years and now facing upside risks from both tariffs and energy prices, the Fed is likely to proceed cautiously and will require clear and sustained evidence that core inflation has moderated and inflation expectations remain anchored. As Chair Powell noted, without visible progress on inflation, cuts are off the table. June now looks too soon. The first cut is more likely in September, if not December, with a second 25bps cut pushed into early 2027.

European Central Bank

Original forecast: Policy on hold through 2026

Revised view: One or two hikes in 2026

With a single mandate focused on price stability, the ECB has less flexibility to tolerate upside inflation risks. Inflation is likely to rise above 3% and remain elevated through year-end.

We now expect one or two rate hikes this year, still fewer than markets price, pushing policy toward the upper end of neutral. While the Governing Council has left the door open to act quickly, we expect the first hike in June rather than as early as April.

Bank of England

Original forecast: Two to three cuts in 2026

Revised view: No change, with upside risk

The BoE also has a single mandate: price stability. The BoE’s unanimous decision to hold rates last week was distinctly hawkish, marking a sharp pivot from prior guidance. Concerns about second-round effects have taken cuts off the table for now. That even the most dovish member supported the decision underscores the depth of inflation concern.

While the labor market would benefit from easing, sticky inflation and renewed geopolitical uncertainty have taken cuts off the table for now. That said, with considerable slack in the UK economy, we doubt higher inflation will translate into persistent core pressure. Market pricing for multiple hikes looks overdone. We expect no change in rates this year, though risks are skewed higher if the conflict intensifies.

Looking ahead: 2027 risks

While weaker growth may not prevent short‑term tightening by the ECB or BoE, we doubt central banks will tighten aggressively or for long. Economic deterioration is likely to reassert itself, with all three central banks resuming rate cuts in 2027 as policy returns toward neutral.

Investor considerations

The Middle East conflict is proving more prolonged and economically disruptive than markets initially assumed. Bond markets have reacted more forcefully than equities as investors reassess inflation persistence and central bank responses. While policymakers are clearly shifting hawkishly, we believe markets are pricing an overly aggressive path for rates.

Forecast uncertainty is unusually high. While a short-term resolution remains possible, a prolonged conflict with sustained energy price pressure would likely elicit a firmer policy response. Investors should expect volatility, remain nimble, and stay diversified.

data.title: Short Term Energy Outlook, March 2026
data.description:
A guest post by D C

The EIA Short Term Energy Outlook (STEO) was published recently. A summary in chart form.

The EIA assumes the oil shock from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz will be over by June and oil prices will fall relatively quickly. This seems an optimistic assumption.

The higher oil prices in 2026 and 2027 lead to higher US crude output than last month’s STEO in 2027 by about 500 kb/d. There is about a 7 month delay between higher prices and the higher output that results in the Permian Basin where most of the US increase is expected to occur. Again I believe this forecast misses the pressure depletion occurring in the Permian Basin that will make it very difficult to even maintain current output levels so this forecast is optimistic in my view at the oil prices assumed by the EIA forecast.

The higher natural gas output compared to previous STEO reports is mostly due to higher associated gas output that is assumed to come from the Permian Basin. Again I think this is incorrect and believe Permian output of both oil and natural gas will be less than forecast by the EIA.

Note that the chart above shows marketed natural gas, before the NGL is removed, where the previous chart gives us dry natural gas production after the NGL has been removed from the wet gas stream. Marketed natural gas output in 2027 is forecast at an average annual rate of 123.9 BCF/d, about 11.6 BCF/d more than dry natural gas average output in 2027.

A colder winter than forecast in October 2025 has led to a lower March 2026 natural gas inventory in the current STEO compared to last October.

Electricity consumption has grown faster than the 2010-2019 period and this is expected to continue through 2027.

Solar generation is expected to grow quickly in 2025-2026 especially in Texas. Wind is the second largest growing category of electricity output nationwide followed by nuclear and hydro for 2025-2026.

Notice the large oil stock build expected in 2026 and 2027. Again this seems optimistic at the forecast price level.

A large OECD stock build is expected in 2026 and 2027, close to pandemic level highs, again very optimistic.

The increase in natural gas output is expected to come from Appalachia, Haynesville, and Permian Basin regions.

Note that for the two charts above the forecast line is set at June 2024 rather than Jan 2026 where it should be placed. The increase in US L48 onshore output comes primarily from the Permian Basin over the August 2026 to Jan 2027 period, with an increase of 300 kb/d over this 5 month period. This forecast is highly unlikely at the oil and gas prices forecast by the March STEO.

Above is my guess for Permian output at an oil and gas price level as forecast by the EIA in the March STEO. This might also be too optimistic in the long run. Average annual Permian tight oil output increases by about 6 kb/d from 2025 to 2027 for this scenario.

data.title: Janus Henderson International Dividend Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 3.62% and the MSCI World ex-USA Index returned 5.20%.

Contributors/detractors

Information technology, healthcare, and consumer discretionary drove positive relative returns, while stock selection in financials and industrials detracted.

Outlook

We continue to see compelling opportunities across sectors and geographies to generate attractive capital and income returns for investors over the medium to long term.

Investment environment

  • International equity markets continued to rise over the final quarter of 2025, buoyed by the loosening of monetary policy by the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) and the easing of global trade tensions.
  • Central banks continued to adopt more accommodative policy, with the Fed cutting interest rates twice, by a combined 50 basis points (bps) to 3.75%. Policymakers said they expect to implement another reduction in 2026. Continued interest in artificial intelligence (AI) initially boosted sentiment, although worries about valuations weighed on share prices in November and December.
  • In the U.S., consumer price rises cooled to 2.7% year on year in November, which was lower than expected compared with September's 3.0% increase. No inflation data was released for October because of the government shutdown. The economy remained resilient, expanding by an annualized 4.3% in the third quarter, although there were some signs of weakness.
  • Both the euro and sterling strengthened slightly against the U.S. dollar during the quarter.
  • Regionally, the UK was the standout performer, buoyed by cooling inflation, looser monetary policy, and reduced tariff worries. The Bank of England lowered its benchmark rate by 25 bps to 3.75% in December. European equities also strengthened.
  • In Asia, equities gained, as investors' AI hopes and a U.S./China trade truce powered regional equities to a record high. Local benchmarks in South Korea and Taiwan also hit record highs, boosted by rallying technology stocks.
  • The financial, healthcare, and material sectors outperformed, while communication services was the only sector to perform negatively. Materials recovered after a weak October due to the strong price rises of some commodities – notably gold, silver, and copper.
  • Overall, the fourth quarter was one of growing confidence and supportive monetary easing.

Portfolio review

Stock selection in technology, healthcare, and consumer discretionary contributed to relative performance over the quarter. Our holdings in pharmaceutical company Roche Holding outperformed, as the U.S. signed deals with pharmaceutical companies to lower tariffs on medicines. In technology, Samsung Electronics Co. and Tokyo Electron Limited were leading contributors, driven by the continued spend on infrastructure required for AI. With consumer spending remaining resilient, certain apparel and luxury goods companies also performed well.

The portfolio's holdings in the industrials and financials sectors underperformed. Holdings in UK data and analytics provider RELX Group and defense manufacturer BAE Systems detracted, with RELX's share price falling due to concerns that AI disruption would impact its services. We think this is unfounded; RELX is better insulated from this risk than what has been reflected in market valuations, especially as it actively deploys AI tools in its operations. Within financials, our holding in Japanese bank Resona Holdings was a key detractor over the period. Share price weakness stemmed from concerns around tighter monetary policy in Japan and a softening outlook for the banking sector. Elsewhere, Canadian telecoms company TELUS Corporation was another key detractor as pricing in Canada has continued to be competitive.

Manager outlook

We enter 2026 with a constructive outlook on equity markets as falling rates and upcoming U.S. and European fiscal stimuli begin to take effect. Lower oil prices are providing a welcome boost to consumers and helping to improve the inflation backdrop in Europe and Asia. AI could be a generational technology leap of which we are in the early stages. While it is yet to deliver major commercial-use cases, and is creating nervousness as a result, the strong potential of AI technology means that corporates and governments may continue to invest behind this theme for a while.

The worst of the tariff policy shock of 2025 now appears to be behind us. Corporate management teams have articulated the impacts of tariffs to their profits, and, with uncertainty subsiding, they may be more open to investing and hiring this year. Positive developments in major Asian economies like China and Japan could lead to better prospects for consumer and commercial spending in that region.

We are monitoring employment and inflation as two indicators that could adversely surprise investors this year. Global markets continue to demonstrate disconnects for valuations and growth expectations between regions and sectors. We expect this will create abundant opportunities for us to deliver attractive income and capital returns for shareholders over the medium to long term.

data.title: Take-Two Interactive: What The Market Is Missing About The GTA VI (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My last ratings for Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) were a hold. And whenever I covered it, even with the changes in earnings, TTWO stock seemed to always maintain a very similar price, something around $230 or $250.

But since my last article (check it here), which was in November, the stock fell 16%, and this even with a fiscal Q3 that was quite solid. In part, the market saw it as a threat and brought down the stock a few months ago due to Alphabet’s (GOOGL) (GOOG) generative AI called Project Genie (check it here), which greatly facilitates the creation of games (with a few prompts, in an ideal and utopian scenario).

I think the effect of this type of tool for Take-Two Interactive is much more positive than negative. But my main point in this article isn't even that. I think the market is missing something very important, which is the pre-order of GTA VI (and the total full potential), which has the potential to collect millions and make TTWO greatly exceed estimates in the coming quarters.

A Word About AI and Rockstar Studios

Rockstar Studios - responsible for GTA - is, arguably, the best game company currently. And I don't say this in terms of financials, but rather in terms of credibility with the community. Companies like Ubisoft (UBSFF) or even CD Projekt RED (OTGLF) have already launched excellent titles, but consumers do not give them the benefit of the doubt because they previously launched games full of bugs or even promised something incredible and did not deliver.

With Rockstar, it's different. It's one of the few companies that fans truly trust in the promises. For instance, even with the delays of GTA VI, I believe most fans believe that this delay was for the good of the game, to come out more polished or with more content.

And this is something difficult to build. Red Dead Redemption 2, which was a game launched in 2018, is used as a benchmark for various factors nowadays. It’s been almost 8 years since its launch, and it still seems that other companies cannot replicate on the same scale what Rockstar did. Of course, there were several other big and good games along the way, but still it is a game that combines several factors; it’s not just a very good narrative with a very well-written script but a living world, where everything seems to connect and the developers truly thought about and cared about several details that resulted in this final work.

This is essential to understanding the game industry nowadays. Returning to Project Genie, maybe competition increases, and companies that are in a low tier manage to produce games faster, etc. But this guarantees nothing. The first point is that players do not want something without a soul.

The perfect illustration of this was Nvidia’s (NVDA) DLSS 5, basically an AI tool that serves to optimize graphics, making them photorealistic. Although I believe it is a powerful tool and that it will advance a lot in the coming years, the community did not receive it well. The feeling for many was that this tool made graphics generic. Okay, it was more realistic, but that doesn't mean it was good. Many times it was still strange; the character didn't seem to demonstrate a feeling that originally the developer wanted him to show. Because of that, in this news, they used a comment as an example: “The obsession with fidelity over art direction is reaching terminal levels.”

So if a company already made generic games and was not good at building worlds, building narratives, and building fun gameplay, it is very likely that in the coming years it will continue performing poorly. In contrast, for companies like Rockstar, which already has the expertise and is the industry benchmark, this means that it will manage to maintain this creative part but accelerate the development process.

It’s not just a matter of IPs, which Rockstar also built over the years. It’s a matter of reputation, a matter of expertise, and also a matter of vision, of reading the industry and delivering what the fans want. It is very different from a corporate decision to prioritize live services (game as a service) just because it is more profitable. What Rockstar did was a bit the opposite of that; first, it created an incredible game (GTA V), and as it saw that there was demand for a service, it built GTA Online.

This is one of the reasons that the market and the community truly believe when Take-Two’s CEO says something like:

“If you have a console and you’re over 17, just explain to me how it is that you’ve decided, ‘no, no GTA VI, not interested?”

In fact, I go a bit further. I don't think the requirement to “have a console” is such a significant barrier here. I myself don't have a console right now, and I am willing to buy one to play GTA VI.

What Market May Be Missing

But what I think the market is missing is not exactly the power of GTA VI. I even think it might be underestimating the GTA VI revenue, but I also think the market is not considering the pre-sale potential.

The following was mentioned in Take-Two's 2013 earnings call:

“Rockstar Games yesterday announced the exciting news that Grand Theft Auto V is planned for worldwide launch during spring 2013. Beginning November 5, fans worldwide can pre-order the title and more information will be revealed in first-look previews releasing throughout November”

In other words, the pre-order started in November, and the game was planned to launch in March of the next year, about 5 months difference.

As Take-Two plans to launch GTA VI, if we assume this window as “optimal,” the pre-order should open in mid-June.

Of course, for this, I am taking as a premise that GTA VI will not be delayed, and although my personal guess is that this really is a reliable launch date, it is difficult to have much conviction in that, and it may again be delayed another few months.

But as November is no longer a date that is far away, maybe what the company is waiting for is the next earnings call to arrive. Take-Two should report results again in the middle of May; that is, it would be a good moment to announce the pre-order, in case the company manages to confirm the launch date for November.

When we see the market estimates, it is noted that in the December 2026 quarter, there is a high expectation for a revenue increase (~$3.5 billion). But for the quarter ending in June 2026, the expectation is quite reasonable, about $1.5 billion, something that Take-Two already delivers normally without GTA VI.

In other articles, I have already argued that a revenue of ~$2 billion (just for GTA VI) in the fiscal year that GTA VI launches is totally feasible and even conservative in a way.

Lately, I tend to believe that even the pre-order should manage to move a few hundred million. The installed base of Sony’s (SONY) PS5 is 90 million. We are not even considering Microsoft’s (MSFT) console here, which would be another few million. I’m also not going to consider that GTA VI will be a catalyst to increase this installed base.

As I mentioned in my old assumptions, thinking about tens of millions of sales in the launch year is quite reasonable, and being honest, considering PC (when it launches), PS5, and XBOX, it doesn't surprise me if this reaches 100,000,000 copies sold. Note that GTA V sold more than that, but also because it was sold for several platforms (PS3, PS4, PS5, and so on).

Anyway, returning to the pre-order, if from this installed base of ~90 million, a minimal percentage, let’s say 2.5% (2.2 million), does the pre-order, and the avg. ticket is $70, it’s $157.5 million additional revenue. These seem like even conservative assumptions to me. The avg. ticket very likely will be higher than $70 with special editions pulling it up, and the cumulative number of pre-orders throughout the year should be much higher than that, since the number for GTA V exceeded 7 million.

So add the ~$1.6 billion in net revenue that Take-Two Interactive is delivering (and most of it is recurring), with some launches like WWE for early 2026, with a few hundred millions from pre-order revenue, and there you go, it is quite easy to have a perspective that in this scenario Take-Two Interactive exceeds the market estimates for the next fiscal quarters, and with all this positive upside in case the pre-order has a lot of demand (5 million copies at $100 dollar avg ticket would be $500 million).

Take-Two Interactive's Valuation

Where I think Take-Two Interactive has now earned an upgrade to a buy rating is in the valuation. Now the margin of safety is better, and I also think one can look at the stock as a “tactical trade” that the market will be surprised by the amount of revenue generated by GTA VI in the launch year.

The market estimates a revenue of $9.2 billion for the 2027 fiscal year. Let's break this down to understand how much the market thinks it will sell.

$1.6 billion multiplied by 4 gives $6.4 billion. This is basically what Take-Two Interactive is delivering now, without GTA VI.

To reach $9.2 billion, $2.8 billion is missing, correct? Well, if GTA VI sells 35 million copies for an $80 avg. ticket, we have exactly $2.8 billion.

This is a very “safe” assumption that the market may have. Selling 35 million copies, which is a very feasible number for this franchise, for $80, which would be only slightly above the minimum market price ($70).

Of course, there is a potential for cannibalization of GTA online revenue in favor of users migrating to GTA VI, but I believe Take-Two Interactive will not wait so long to launch the new GTA online so that revenue remains recurring. If this happens, the $80 avg. ticket is conservative. Not only because there should be demand for deluxe editions, but because many should buy some subscription or money in GTA online.

Besides that, believing that GTA VI will sell 45 million in the year it launches is not being too optimistic. It is a scenario that can truly happen. As I mentioned, it will require that half of the PS5 installed base buy the game. We are not even counting Xbox, nor are we considering the CEO's assumption that “every adult with a console will buy the game.” 45 million copies for $80 is already an addition of $3.6 billion in revenue. This basically can make Take-Two reach something close to $10 billion in revenue per year.

For a company with an enterprise value of ~$38 billion, it really isn't as if it were a bargain, but it already seems attractive enough to me. The operating cash flow margin for the 2026 fiscal year should be around ~7%. But with operating leverage, Take-Two Interactive's free cash flow margin has already reached 30% in some years.

With a potential of ~$3 billion in free cash flow, the EV-to-FCF is 12x at the moment. Certainly not a bargain, since in mid-2014 (post GTA V launch) the P/FCF multiple was closer to 3x.

But the market had not yet realized, and Take-Two Interactive had also not proven itself as this recurring revenue company. With GTA VI, this can be again confirmed. That is, with a new GTA online, and now with the expertise of taking care of this type of business, not to mention that GTA VI will be sold later for PC for a new PS6 (perhaps it will even be sold on PS7 and on a new Nintendo console when it emerges), in other words, the potential to maintain revenue at higher levels exists.

Also, since the market values other game stocks like Capcom (CCOEY), Roblox (RBLX), and CD Projekt RED at 15x EBITDA+, it’s pretty easy to justify paying 12x EBITDA for TTWO.

This means that there is a scenario where the free cash flow yield of TTWO maintains (with certain oscillations, obviously) at something close to 8%.

Risks and Bottom Line

Well, my upgrade here is considering this scenario I mentioned. I think maybe the stock sees better moments and has a re-rating again when the market perceives that AI is not a threat, as well as perceives that GTA VI pre-orders can add much more to revenue than they are estimating. And of course, the quality of the company is very good, so in the long term, Take-Two Interactive can be a quality compounder.

But even so, I need to mention the risks. My conviction for the short term is high, but for the long term it gets much more cloudy. That is, let’s assume that Take-Two Interactive truly manages to deliver something close to $3 billion in free cash flow (not necessarily that, but close). If it doesn't implement the online mode of GTA very well, this will not be sustained for many years; it will be something fleeting that gives a peak in the chart and then normalizes.

And with Red Dead Redemption 2, Rockstar managed to fail in the online mode, so it is something that can repeat. This would be disastrous, because maybe not only would it burn this opportunity to have an environment with a lot of engagement, maybe even create something close to a "metaverse" like Roblox is doing, but it could even affect the current revenue of GTA 5 online. Besides that, much of the optionality, like a remake of the old GTAs in the new GTA 6 engine, can be executed well or can flop totally.

In any case, as I believe the opportunities are much greater than the risks, and the margin of safety is already starting to be minimally enough, my rating is a cautious buy for TTWO stock.

data.title: Fidelity Select Communication Services Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The communication services sector gained 3.50% in the fourth quarter, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Communication Services 25/50 (Media Linked) Index, as the broad stock market extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024.

Communication services stocks outpaced the 2.66% gain of the broad-based S&P 500® index and had one of that index's strongest sector gains, trailing only health care. The sector's result was driven in good part by companies benefiting from the boom in generative AI development. Against this backdrop, interactive media & services, which represented more than half of the MSCI sector index in Q4, gained 11% and had a weighty influence on the index's overall result. This subindustry includes Google parent firm Alphabet (+38%), which by itself represented roughly 27% of the sector index. The past three months, Alphabet continued to execute well on its artificial intelligence strategy while producing solid financial results across its other businesses.

Alternative carriers (+25%) stocks also advanced strongly, led by satellite manufacturer AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) (+48%). Shares of the company pushed higher in early December ahead of its next-generation satellite launch, designed to bring broadband access directly to cellular phone users worldwide. Elsewhere, the broadcasting subindustry rose 5%, propelled by Fox (FOXA) (+15%).

Conversely, the interactive home entertainment segment returned about -17%, hurt in part by results for Roblox (-41%). Shares of the online gaming company, which uses AI for content creation, moderation and user experience enhancement, declined after the company reported slowing user growth, increases in infrastructure investment, and margin compression, mainly due to payouts to video-game developers.

In general, defensive areas of the sector lagged. Wireless telecommunication services returned -13%, hampered by T-Mobile (-15%). Integrated telecommunication services (-7%) also struggled, with index components Verizon and AT&T (T) returning -6% and -11%, respectively. Lastly, in cable & satellite (-6%), Charter Communications (-24%) and Comcast (-4%) underperformed, as subscriber growth among the cable providers remained tepid.

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 2.91%, trailing the 3.50% advance of the MSCI U.S. IMI Communication Services 25/50 (Media Linked) Index but edging the 2.66% result of the broad-based S&P 500® index. Importantly, given our focus, longer-term performance comparisons favor the fund over the benchmark.

Stock selection detracted from the fund's result versus the MSCI sector index. Specifically, picks in the interactive media & services and interactive home entertainment industries weighed on relative performance. An underweight in the strong-performing alternative carriers group also hurt.

An overweight stake in the aforementioned Roblox was the biggest individual relative detractor. We reduced the holding after the company missed financial expectations in Q3 and scaled back its forward-looking guidance. A larger-than-index position in Meta Platforms (-10%) also hurt. In late October, the parent of Facebook and Instagram reported record quarterly revenue, but net income of $2.7 billion was much lower than expected, largely due to high spending on AI infrastructure. The stock pulled back, but we increased the position, taking a longer-term view.

In advertising, shares of digital advertising platform Magnite (MGNI) (-25%) declined after delivering a strong result the previous quarter. The company provides technology that allows digital content publishers to sell personalized advertising across various connected channels. Despite announcing an increase in revenue in Q3, the firm said it anticipated a slowdown in Q4, suggesting that macroeconomic pressure might lead to weaker spending on advertising.

In contrast, non-index exposure to the semiconductors industry added the most value, led by SK Hynix (+82%). The stock of the South Korea-headquartered firm rallied amid strong demand for its digital memory solutions. In a third-quarter conference call with analysts, the company said that all of its memory products – including DRAM, NAND and high-bandwidth memory chips – were sold out through 2026. Given the stock's strong run, we trimmed the fund's position.

Elsewhere, avoiding major index component Verizon Communications (-6%) also contributed. The integrated telecom services company continued to struggle with anemic subscriber growth and high costs for upgrades to its dense network, which have added to the company's already-high debt levels. ■

Outlook and Positioning

As we begin 2026, the fund is positioned in stocks of companies that we believe have a sustainable competitive moat and can deliver durable growth.

Position sizing in the portfolio is driven by fundamental analysis on a company-by-company basis and in-depth research of key trends in the communication services sector and is based on our conviction – and the differentiation of our view – relative to the market's assessment of a company's prospects, as reflected by its price. We like to invest when we believe a company's rate of growth is either mispriced or underestimated, and we are willing to pay a premium for faster and/or durable growth.

The fund's foundational investment themes remain growth in broadband and the shift to digital. As of year-end, the portfolio had a sizable underweight in cable & satellite, a segment that is challenged by continued subscriber losses and increased competition from providers of fixed wireless services. The portfolio is also notably underweight in the integrated telecommunication services group, which is characterized by slow growth.

These underweights reflect our preference for investing in faster-growing areas. The fund primarily holds U.S.-based firms, but we do choose companies based in foreign countries when they meet our investment criteria. Also, the fund owns positions in application-based companies that are leveraging wireless resources to bring services to consumers. This category includes Amazon.com (AMZN), the fund's largest overweight and No. 5 position at the end of 2025.

For the past several years, we have witnessed the rise of streaming services and demand for high-quality video content that platforms can leverage to compete for consumers' attention and help increase subscribers. This content ranges from blockbuster television series and movies to live sports entertainment. In line with this theme, the fund holds several content platforms and developers, including Warner Bros. Discovery, Disney and Liberty Media, the parent of Formula One Group.

As of December 31, the fund mainly owns stocks of companies we believe can capitalize on the world going digital. Trends such as rising engagement with mobile apps, streaming and interactive entertainment, direct-response advertising, demand for faster broadband, and adoption of 5G and cloud services remain prominent areas of investment. Additionally, companies that are emerging as leading beneficiaries from the adoption of AI are among the fund's largest holdings. Looking ahead, we are optimistic about the long-term prospects for these areas, and we see a significant runway for certain communication services companies to create value greater than we believe is currently reflected in share prices.

That said, we are closely watching shifts in the macroeconomic environment. If consumer confidence and spending pull back, digital advertising could suffer, hindering revenue for companies in key segments of the sector. Regardless of the market environment, we intend to focus on companies that we think show potential for long-term revenue and earnings growth, and we try to buy a stock when its price is dislocated from the company's true value. ■

Fidelity® Select Communication Services Portfolio

Investment Approach

• Fidelity® Select Communication Services Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.

• We employ a bottom-up, stock-by-stock approach to capitalize on our view that growth and revisions to earnings and free cash flow per share drive communication services stocks. As such, the fund tends to emphasize companies with sustainable growth that is likely to beat expectations, as well as secular growers that can drive consistent excess returns.

• We use fundamental analysis, supported by Fidelity's deep and experienced global technology, media and telecommunication team, to identify stocks that we believe have the greatest dislocation between long-term earnings-growth potential and valuation.

data.title: Alimentation Couche-Tard Inc.: I Am Staying Bullish As Underlying Fundamentals Remain Sound
data.description:
Investment Action

I upgraded Alimentation Couche-Tard Inc. (ATD:CA) to a buy rating previously, as I thought the setup looked much better than in Q1 2026, with better merchandise growth, stable fuel margins, and also the restart of capital returns to shareholders. The Q3 2026 results showed us better US same-store merchandise growth, stronger fuel margins across all geographies, continued traction in food, and improving demand across other categories like nicotine and energy drinks. All of these make the earnings growth story look better than I thought.

Latest Quarter Results

Total revenue for Q3 2026 saw a growth of 4.3% y/y, coming in at $21.8 billion. Within it, merchandise and service revenue was $5.8 billion, up 8.7%, while road transportation fuel revenue was $15.9 billion, up 2.8%. Other revenue was $196.5 million, up 2.2%. Inside the store, same-store merchandise revenue grew 2.8% in the US, 0.4% in Europe and other regions, and 0.3% in Canada, which drove consolidated same-store merchandise growth to 2%. In road transportation fuel, same-store volumes fell 0.4% in the US and 1.6% in Europe and other regions, while Canada grew 4.2%. Merchandise and service gross profit was $2 billion, up 8.6%, while road transportation fuel gross profit was $2.17 billion, up 16.9%. By region, US merchandise and service gross profit came in at $1.32 billion, up 7.9%; Europe and other regions saw $464.6 million, up 14.9%, while Canada saw $218 million, up 1.1%. As for road transportation, US fuel gross profit was $1.39 billion, up 15.2%. Europe and other regions saw $574.2 million, up 18.8%, and Canada saw $205.5 million, up 23.6%. Although the headline revenue growth figure was not exciting, total gross profit performance was great, growing by 12.5% y/y to $4.24 billion. Combined with lower opex growth, EBIT came in at $1.14 billion, up 18.2%. This translated into ~19% adj. EPS growth.

US Convenience Recovery and Fuel Margins

Based on the Q3 numbers, we now have more evidence that the US convenience segment is recovering nicely. The cleanest datapoint is the 2.8% US same-store merchandise growth in Q3 2026, which is the strongest performance in more than two years. Importantly, this was not due to any seasonal or one-off events. The growth came from several core categories, including energy, nicotine, and food. Management also specifically highlighted that nearly all business units saw positive same-store sales as the quarter progressed, and traffic was up in almost half of them. The keywords here are "as the quarter progressed," which means ATD:CA likely exited Q3 with higher than 2.8%, setting up well for Q4 growth to accelerate. This is a big takeaway because in the last quarter, one could still argue that the improvement in same-store sales was due to the successful value offer. That doubt should now be off the table, as strength is seen across more categories, more business units, and more markets within the US store base.

I think an important reason why ATD:CA was able to achieve this was also because the food program continues to work. For those that missed my previous update, refer to the quote below to understand the impact. The momentum here remains strong, with US food same-store sales growing in the mid-to-high single digits. ATD:CA also sold 13.3 million meal deal bundles in the quarter, which reinforces my point that bundling helps to drive transactions. And note that food same-store sales are higher than the convenience segment, which implies it is an important traffic driver.

We also know from the numbers that the US road transportation fuel segment continues to show resilience as gross margin actually went up to 47.71 cents per gallon, up 3.43 cents y/y. The same trend is seen in Europe and other regions and Canada as well, which all also saw y/y growth. Given that fuel is still a large part of the earnings base, with fuel margins healthy while inside sales improve, I think the setup for Q4 and the coming quarters remains solid.

Here is a better way to illustrate this. By bundling meal deals with higher-value categories, like, say, energy drinks, for example, which saw mid-teens growth partly from meal deal attachment, ATD:CA is effectively turning this food purchase occasion into a larger basket purchase occasion. This is what you want to see, as it drives the flywheel of more footfall leading to the purchase of other merchandise lines, which leads to overall SSS growth.

And food like anything, it's driving increased transactions, and increased transactions are good. We continue to grow that percentage. Alex Miller, Q2 transcript

Note that the higher-value categories carry higher margins, so when ATD:CA bundles with food, it can effectively use them to "subsidize" the food product to attract more consumers (i.e., bring the price down to capture value-conscious consumers). Overall, margins should grow, and indeed they have. US merchandise and service gross margin expanded 90 bps y/y to 34.7%.

Broad Category Growth and Supply Chain

Besides food, demand is also picking up in other product segments, which further derisks the earnings growth story. Take nicotine, for instance. In the US, nicotine same-store sales grew in the mid-to-high single digits, and cigarettes also returned to growth. Energy drinks were also strong, with strong mid-teens growth across all three regions. Management even mentioned that energy drinks are the main driver for packaged beverage growth in the US. Taking a step back and looking at the entire landscape, ATD is getting a very good demand response from customers across more of its stores, and that is a very healthy demand signal.

I should also touch on the supply chain side of things. In Q3, ATD:CA opened 37 new-to-industry stores and had 58 more under construction at quarter-end. It has also opened three new distribution centers and now supports ~3,200 stores across North America with self-distribution from six facilities. I thought this move to strengthen the supply chain made a lot of sense, especially with demand improving. My logic is that with more control over supply, ATD:CA will have more control over the cost of goods, product availability, and assortment. In other words, it gives more control over what ATD:CA can offer in store. For sure, those new distribution centers will weigh on near-term profit margins, but that shouldn't be the focus. All reinvestments weigh on profits, and the long-term trade-off looks worth it since it strengthens ATD:CA's business model.

Valuation

ATD:CA share price moved upwards as I expected, touching a high of ~$86 before it tumbled down to the recent $70+ (which I think is due to the US/Iran conflict, where investors are worried about LNG and oil supply chain disruption). The fact of the matter is that the demand recovery momentum has continued, with fuel margins stronger across all geographies in Q3. Food is also becoming a larger and more durable traffic driver. Most importantly, ~adj. EPS grew 19% y/y, which is way higher than the 10+% CAGR that consensus modeled (as I noted previously). This gives comfort that low-teens adj. EPS growth should continue to be doable in the coming years if this momentum continues.

The discount gap between ATD:CA and the SPX index has also closed, now back to near historical average, so I wouldn't be expecting any strong upwards multiple re-rating in the near term. But that is fine. At the current 18x NTM PE, it implies a share price target of ~USD$64.6 based on FY28 estimates (low-teens adj. EPS CAGR), or CAD88.86 per share.

Risk

Given that ATD:CA is scaling up its supply chain while demand is growing, the risk comes down to execution. With multiple programs ongoing (food, loyalty, supply chain, and the Core + More program), if that results in management losing focus on driving demand, earnings growth could be a lot worse than expected since costs are piling up (in the near term). I also think category mix is worth monitoring. Cigarettes returned to growth in Q3 2026, but that mix is not ideal if ATD wants better inside economics over time.

Conclusion

I stay bullish on ATD:CA. The Q3 quarter showed that demand recovery is continuing, with healthy fuel margins and better store execution. Food continues to do well, other categories are also contributing, and management is investing to strengthen the supply chain for the long run. The valuation may not leave room for a major re-rating from here, but with low-teen adj. EPS growth, I still see upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IWMI: Still Solid, But Less Compelling Now
data.description:
In July 2025, I had rated the NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) a Buy. The reasoning was simple. I liked IWMI's ability to monetize small cap volatility while keeping equity participation relatively intact. This was despite NAV erosion risks and some upside capping structural limitations that are known to be associated with covered call option strategies.

IWMI has played out exceedingly well since, with a total return of ~17% at the start of the year, before markets started to correct. Importantly, this strong performance was supported by IWM performing better than the S&P 500 since the end of last year. IWMI was able to capture a vast majority of this rally (despite theoretical concerns around its upside capturing limitations) and, in fact, added an expected couple of percentage points in the recent small cap (proxied by IWM) drawdown too.

This thesis reviews IWMI's case in income portfolios as we enter a new regime of slower growth and even pressures on the small-cap pack. My argument now points to a Hold, with any incremental capital better deployed in larger cap option strategies. The reasoning is this. Since IWMI prioritizes upside capture over income (as seen in the way it captured the rally till the turn of the year), it will not aggressively add premium income if small caps stay rangebound. And if small caps correct more aggressively (a more likely scenario), then income from option premiums will not be sufficient to offset the capital loss of the underlying portfolio, leading to NAV erosion.

The Regime Outlook

By definition, the small-cap space is more volatile and dispersed. Earnings are not as stable as the larger cap space. That makes it a great underlying for premium harvesting, but requires either a bullish trend or drawdowns that tend to mean revert quickly.

I have been recommending Buys in general for covered calls based on the large cap space of late (if everything else in terms of intrinsic methodology lines up) because the regime ahead looks more balanced. The AI trade is unwinding to an extent at high valuations - cooling from a rally over the past couple of years.

On the upside, I see capex pressures will weigh over the next few quarters, earnings revisions slow down, and markets wait for more clarity to emerge on the AI economics and returns on capex. In some pockets, like SaaS, there is a greater uncertainty on how AI actually changes the landscape and where pricing power eventually lands.

However, I do not see a great downside risk either because the AI tailwind is indeed secular and multi-year (even if over cycles maybe). Volatility has not reached panic levels like it did in April last year - even then it did not sustain, and markets worked through that sell-off to greater highs eventually. The setup points to more time corrections from here or a slower recovery. In such a consolidation phase, covered calls, if set up well, can add meaningful incremental income from premium harvesting, especially if volatility remains moderately high (not capital destructive net-net).

The IWMI underlying is less pressured due to valuations like the AI trade - unwinding in the AI space does not impact small caps directly as much. But the offsetting impact of the regime ahead is where IWMI's underlying thesis suffers. The dominant theme for small caps ahead is rates and financing conditions, more than valuations. Higher real yields directly hit interest expenses and open up refinancing risks. That balance sheet sensitivity could be worse than the valuation pressures we are seeing in the large-cap space. Especially if we are talking about stagflation ahead with input costs elevated (proxied by oil prices and hence inflation) with growth remaining subdued. At the moment, small caps look more exposed to correction risks as rates stay higher for longer or if market sentiments remain negative. Which means the bullish or range-bound trends that worked so far for IWMI may be missing ahead (unlike in the case of large-cap covered call strategies).

IWMI's Mechanics

IWMI's mechanics show why the thesis on the small-cap universe trumps the intrinsic setup. IWMI is generally a less aggressive option strategy that does not aggressively write options on the entire underlying holdings and at aggressive strikes. That is, the emphasis is on being able to keep upside capture as open as possible, not income generation.

A downloaded the current holdings (as of March 23rd, 2026) from the ETF website and saw two covered call positions. Both were positioned for April 17th expiry on the Russell 200 index. The strikes for both the call writing legs (715 short contracts each) are at 2685 and 2740, currently around 9-10% away from the spot prices. This may be due to the corrections seen recently and not an indicator of the way IWMI sets up strikes versus spots, but it is important to note that such positions are not adjusted closer to the spot even if the premium left to harvest in these far OTM strikes is not meaningful anyway and weeks are left before expiry.

A more conclusive proof of how IWMI does not maximize premium harvesting is seen in terms of notional coverage of these option positions. At 715 short contracts for each leg at around the 2700 strike, the exposure for both legs combined accounts for around $380m in notional terms (applying a multiplier of $100 per contract). That is around 50-60% of the underlying holdings of ~$650m.

When you keep almost half the portfolio not covered by option writing, you end up seeing a trajectory in terms of total returns that is very close to the underlying holding itself with minor income additions on corrections or consolidation. That is what we see from July last year to early 2026. This is why IWMI is more reliant on the thesis for the underlying (whether the Russell 2000 is bullish or not) than its own option layer mechanics. And that is also why it becomes a Buy in bullish conditions for the underlying. Income generation is not strong even if we see a consolidation, and in the case of IWMI, the risks of a correction from here in the Russell 2000 overshadow the benefits of owning the covered call layer as such.

The total return charts not only show how IWMI closely tracks IWM but also explain why it has done exceedingly well in comparison with covered call strategies based on the S&P 500 so far - that is, IWM has outperformed SPY. That is about to change. I feel the thesis is more favorable for the broader markets at this stage, and options add the value expected in income strategies in the space. For small caps, especially ones like IWMI (where the underlying thesis is even more important), we could see IWMI save a few percentage points versus the underlying index, but overall total returns will likely be lower because of index risks.

Overall, IWMI is best rated a Hold. It remains a strong income vehicle for very long-term investors who believe in the small-cap growth thesis, but we could easily see several quarters where large cap based covered call strategies do better. In my opinion, IWMI will only be upgraded to a Buy again if the market outlook improves. For now incremental capital is better deployed elsewhere.
